TextENGNew: (...What could possibly be so amusing about all this?)
An elderly relative had asked me to help serve at the banquet, and... I could not understand the source of their boundless joy.
changeReason: Changes 'his extreme joy' (singular male) to 'their boundless joy' (plural), aligning with the JPN which does not specify gender and refers to the villagers collectively.

TextENGNew: No, it was not that I "didn't understand"...
It was that I "couldn't comprehend" it.
"Three cheers for the successful defeat of the Hinamizawa dam project!!"
"Three cheers for the Onigafuchi Defense Alliance!!"
changeReason: Changes group name from 'Onigafuchi Guardians' to 'Onigafuchi Defense Alliance,' a more literal translation of 鬼ヶ淵死守同盟 (Defense Alliance).

TextENGNew: The villagers raised toast after toast, their faces flushed red, gorging on food, drowning in drink, and making a deafening racket.
Every last one of them was slapping one another on the back with booming laughter, shaking hands indiscriminately with whoever happened to be nearby.
changeReason: Changes 'patting one another on the shoulder' to 'slapping one another on the back'; JPN 肩を叩き合った specifically refers to shoulders, not backs.

TextENGNew: The night had grown late... but far from dying down, the fervor only kept climbing higher.
"Ga ha ha ha ha!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_farmMa1017. Serves you right, you bureaucrats!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_farmMa1018.Now you know the power of all Hinamizawa standing united!"

"That's right, that's right!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_farmMb1017. Justice was on our side!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_farmMb1018. As if we'd let those self-serving government officials destroy the foundation of our lives!"
"A toast to our justice, and to Oyashiro-sama!!"
The old folks raised yet another toast—who could even count how many times by now—holding up their brimming glasses of beer with a shout.
...The laughter grew louder still, ringing in my ears until they ached.
The room was thick with the mingled stench of alcohol and food, and feeling slightly ill, I stepped out into the hallway.
"...Haah."
Finally escaping that suffocating atmosphere, I drew a deep, long breath.
The Hinamizawa district was no longer going to be submerged beneath a dam reservoir.
For the older generation, not losing the village where they were born and raised was surely cause for celebration.
But... was truly "everyone" in the village happy about this?
The reason that question had come to mind was because I had overheard my parents' conversation.
changeReason: Changes 'listening to my parents' to 'overheard my parents' conversation,' more accurately reflecting 立ち聞き (eavesdropping) in the JPN.

TextENGNew: "...Apparently the government is offering quite generous compensation to families displaced from Hinamizawa.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_natsumiM1002. Enough to buy a house in a reasonably urban area."
"Ahh... if I had known, I would have kept my residence registered in Hinamizawa."
"Hey... stop that.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_natsumiF1002. Mother would be furious if she heard you say that.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_natsumiF1003. We are, at least nominally, members of the Kimiyoshi family."
changeReason: Adds 'at least nominally' to reflect JPN 仮にも (even if only in name), which the old translation omitted. Also changes 'your mother' to 'Mother' (referring to mother-in-law).

TextENGNew: "I don't care.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_natsumiM1005. Even if we're part of the Three Families, we're just a branch family.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_natsumiM1006. We never got any special benefits from it, and for me, it was nothing but an inconvenient backwater."
...That was why she had gone to a boarding school in the city for high school.
She found her college and a job in the same town, and even during summer breaks, she used part-time work and club activities as excuses to almost never return home.
changeReason: Adds 'club activities' which the old translation omitted; JPN 部活だの、バイトだの mentions both club activities and part-time work.

TextENGNew: That was why, even now... she and Grandma did not get along well.
When the anti-dam protests erupted, Mom refused to participate at all, not wanting to be seen alongside Grandma and her obsessive attachment to Hinamizawa.
In the first place, Mom had always disliked living in the countryside—not just Hinamizawa.
changeReason: New translation captures JPN 雛見沢に限らず (not limited to Hinamizawa), specifying she disliked countryside life in general, not just Hinamizawa specifically.

TextENGNew: Limited entertainment, scarce information.
Inconvenient transportation, a stifling provincial atmosphere...
changeReason: Old translates 辛気臭い風土 as 'the climate was hot' (physical weather) when it means 'a stifling provincial atmosphere' (social environment). Mistranslation of 風土.

TextENGNew: Mom could be loud and bothersome at times, but... at least on that point, I truly understood how she felt.
Because... I was not particularly fond of Hinamizawa either.
And after tonight, that feeling was tilting sharply toward outright dislike.
changeReason: Changes 'hate' to 'outright dislike'; the JPN 嫌い is literally 'dislike/hate,' but the old translation used 'hate' in quotes while the new softens to 'dislike,' slightly shifting the implied intensity.

TextENGNew: "...@k@vS20/36/VTK1_natsumi1004.People have died.@k@vS20/36/VTK1_natsumi1005. How can these people laugh like this...?"
It had happened just last month.
The on-site manager at the dam construction site had been brutally murdered by his workers... and his body was found dismembered.
One of the culprits still had not been caught... and there were rumors he was hiding somewhere in Okinomiya.
Because of that, my school had mandated group commutes—going to and from school together.
But then... word came to the town council that the Hinamizawa dam construction plan was being put up for re-evaluation.
And so the villagers had come to the conclusion that the on-site manager's death was Oyashiro-sama's curse... and that the government had frozen the dam project out of fear.
"But... how can they rejoice this much?@k@vS20/36/VTK1_natsumi1007. Even if things turned out well for them, is it right to celebrate when someone else has suffered such a terrible fate...?"
When I once saw the on-site manager on the road, he certainly had a rough appearance... and he frightened me at first.
But when he asked me for directions and I pointed the way, he said "Thanks, young miss" and bought me a can of juice.
I had lived in Hinamizawa for a few years before, and... everyone had always thought of me as a gloomy girl, so the villagers were always somewhat distant toward me.
That was why being shown such simple kindness, after so long... made me genuinely happy.
(You really can't judge a person by appearance.@k@vS20/36/VTK1_natsumi1009. I'm sure he's actually a good person...)
That was precisely why it had bothered me when Mion-chan and the others, with whom I was somewhat close, kept badmouthing him.
I had no desire to join in the protest demonstrations organized by relatives and villagers.
I knew people were whispering behind my back about how improper it was for someone from the Three Families... but I pretended not to hear.
But... that man... had died just last month.
He was killed.
I could never speak with him again.
changeReason: Changes 'He'd never speak again' to 'I could never speak with him again'; the JPN もう話ができない implies the inability to converse together, not just that he cannot speak.

TextENGNew: I would never run into him on the road and have him buy me juice again.
Thinking about that... my chest constricted with a grief that was almost unbearable.
And yet... despite all of that, these people were...
It was not just their callousness that repulsed me.
Oryou-san and Grandpa Kimiyoshi seemed to be genuinely rejoicing, practically gloating "serves them right"... but the people around them were only pretending to be happy.
changeReason: Changes 'Uncle Kimiyoshi' to 'Grandpa Kimiyoshi'; JPN 公由のおじーちゃん uses おじいちゃん (grandpa), not おじさん (uncle).

TextENGNew: The proof was in how, whenever one of them slipped away from the circle of cheers to fetch another drink, they would sigh repeatedly with a strangely weary expression.
Their celebration was not genuine... they were only going along with it to avoid being left out.

They did not want to defy the will of the village the way the murdered manager had... That was it—they were merely performing enthusiasm as a desperate excuse to show the others they were on the right side.
"...How pathetic."
The truth was that nobody... actually believed in the curse.
What they truly feared was straying from the village's consensus... being branded an "enemy" of Hinamizawa.
They feared the "people" who could commit barbaric acts without flinching—feared being hated, despised, and ultimately killed as a "curse" to make an example of them.
changeReason: Old reverses the direction: JPN describes fearing people who commit barbaric acts and being killed as a 'curse,' but old says villagers would show barbaric faces and wish a curse would kill others.

TextENGNew: ...And the on-site manager, deemed deserving of the "curse," was the one killed this time.
If that was how it worked, then the next most likely targets of the "curse" would be the Houjou family, who had organized the pro-dam faction.
Or perhaps someone who had sided with them... or even someone who had simply failed to declare loudly enough whether they were friend or foe...
I had heard villagers spreading rumors like that on several occasions when I was pressed into helping with the fieldwork.
And each time, it became clearer that what they feared—so deeply they could barely bring themselves to name it—was not "Oyashiro-sama."
changeReason: New translation adds 'so deeply they could barely bring themselves to name it,' capturing JPN 言葉にするのもはばかるほど (so fearful they hesitated even to speak of it).

TextENGNew: It was Oyashiro-sama's earthly proxies: the "Three Families."
changeReason: New translation adds 'Oyashiro-sama's earthly proxies,' reflecting JPN その代弁者たる (those who speak on behalf of [Oyashiro-sama]), clarifying the Three Families' role as proxies rather than just 'leaders.'

TextENGNew: "Everyone... is a liar."
They spoke fervently of love for their homeland and bonds of fellowship.
...But at the root of it all was "fear."
Could solidarity born from something so shameful and cruel truly be called just and legitimate? Was that really something to take pride in...?
...My head ached.
My heart was pounding wildly, and cold sweat trickled down my cheeks.
Pressing a hand to my chest, I decided it was time to head home, and went looking for Grandpa Kimiyoshi...
changeReason: Changes 'Uncle Kimiyoshi' to 'Grandpa Kimiyoshi'; JPN 公由のおじーちゃん uses おじいちゃん (grandpa).
